title: LO1 — Explain in your own words: what problem does this approach solve?

children.body:
One of the first learning objectives asks us to articulate the core problem that the techniques in Unit 1 are designed to solve. Before looking back at the slides, try writing a two or three sentence explanation here. What is the problem, and why do existing alternatives fall short?

I'll start: the problem as I understand it is that naive approaches don't scale because they recompute shared subproblems repeatedly. The techniques in Unit 1 trade memory for time by storing intermediate results. I'm less clear on when that tradeoff stops being worthwhile.

Adding to what I wrote: I looked at the lecture again and the key insight seems to be that the subproblem graph is a DAG, which is what makes it safe to store and reuse results. If the graph had cycles the approach would break. That rules out a class of problems where the approach can't be applied directly.
Thanks, that's a clearer explanation than what I had.

Happy to expand. The DAG structure also explains why topological ordering matters — you have to solve subproblems in an order that guarantees their dependencies are already resolved. Bottom-up iteration respects that order naturally, which is why it's often preferred over the recursive version even when memoization would be equivalent in terms of calls.
Good thread. I'd like to hear from others — user-1 has laid out one reading. Does anyone see it differently, or have an example that challenges the DAG characterisation?

One edge case: if you introduce state reset between calls you can handle some cyclic graphs, but at that point you've stepped outside the standard framework. I think that's why the lecture was careful to scope the claim to acyclic structures.
